cards.back: A combination of physical RAM and secondary storage (hard disk or solid-state drive) used to extend the address space of a computer, allowing it to handle more memory than is physically available.
A memory management strategy that divides the memory into fixed-size blocks called pages, each of which is stored in a separate area of physical memory or secondary storage.

A memory management strategy that divides the memory into fixed-size blocks called segments, each of which is associated with a specific program or data, and each is stored in a separate area of physical memory or secondary storage.

A memory management strategy that moves data from physical RAM to secondary storage (such as a hard disk or solid-state drive) when the system needs more memory, and brings it back when it is needed again.
A memory management strategy that combines the benefits of paging and segmentation, where a program requests access to memory only when it needs it, and the operating system takes care of the rest.
